B:
For internal microservices, my default recommendation is **gRPC for service-to-service calls, with REST kept at the system's edge**. But the honest answer is that it depends on a few things about your team and traffic. Let me give you the recommendation and the reasoning, then the cases where I'd flip.

## Recommendation: gRPC internally, REST at the edge

Use gRPC for the synchronous internal calls between your own services, and expose REST (or GraphQL) at the public/gateway boundary where browsers, third parties, and humans need to reach you.

## Why gRPC wins *internally*

**Schema-first contracts.** Protobuf forces an explicit `.proto` contract that both sides compile against. You get generated clients/servers in every language, and breaking changes tend to surface at build time rather than as a 2 a.m. production deserialization error. With REST+JSON, the contract is a document (OpenAPI) that the code can silently drift away from.

**Performance.** Binary protobuf over HTTP/2 is meaningfully smaller and faster to (de)serialize than JSON over HTTP/1.1. HTTP/2 multiplexing means many concurrent calls share one connection instead of head-of-line blocking. For chatty internal meshes doing thousands of calls per request fan-out, this compounds.

**Streaming as a first-class citizen.** Bidirectional and server streaming are built in. Doing this over REST means SSE, long-polling, or bolting on WebSockets.

**Cross-language consistency.** If you have Go, Java, Python, Node services talking to each other, codegen gives you consistent, type-safe stubs everywhere instead of hand-maintained client libraries.

## Why REST stays at the edge

- **Browsers** can't speak raw gRPC without gRPC-Web + a proxy.
- **Debuggability**: `curl`, Postman, and browser devtools work out of the box. JSON is human-readable in logs.
- **Ecosystem**: caching, CDNs, API gateways, auth middleware, and third-party tooling all assume HTTP/REST semantics.
- **Lower onboarding cost** for external consumers — nobody wants to compile your protos to call your public API.

## When I'd flip to REST internally too

Pick REST/JSON for internal services if:

- **Your team is small and the services are few.** The operational overhead of gRPC (proto tooling, build pipeline, the HTTP/2 + load-balancing gotchas — gRPC needs L7-aware LB because it pins long-lived connections) isn't worth it for a handful of services.
- **Traffic is low** and latency/payload size aren't real constraints. The performance win is irrelevant if you're doing hundreds of requests per minute.
- **Your team already has deep REST muscle memory** and no polyglot pressure. Velocity matters more than marginal efficiency.

## The third option worth naming

For **asynchronous** internal communication, don't force either one — use an event/message bus (Kafka, NATS, SQS). A lot of "should it be REST or gRPC" tension dissolves once you realize the call didn't need to be synchronous at all. Reserve both REST and gRPC for genuine request/response.

---

**Bottom line:** Polyglot stack, many services, performance-sensitive fan-out, or you value enforced contracts → **gRPC internally, REST at the edge.** Small team, few services, low traffic, strong existing REST habits → **REST everywhere** and revisit later. Either way, push as much cross-service communication as you can onto async events.

If you tell me your stack, team size, and roughly how chatty the services are, I'll turn this into a concrete call.
G:
**Recommendation: Default to gRPC for service-to-service calls, keep REST at the edge.** For internal microservices where you control both sides, gRPC is the better default. Use REST/JSON for anything external-facing or browser-facing.

## Why gRPC for internal calls

- **Schema-first contracts.** Protobuf gives you a single source of truth with generated clients/servers in every language. This is the biggest win — it kills a whole class of "the client and server disagree about the payload" bugs and makes breaking changes visible at compile time.
- **Performance.** Binary serialization + HTTP/2 multiplexing means lower latency and CPU than JSON-over-HTTP/1.1. Matters most on hot paths and high fan-out call graphs.
- **Streaming.** First-class bidirectional/server/client streaming, which REST handles awkwardly (SSE, long-polling, chunked hacks).
- **Code generation.** Less hand-written boilerplate and serialization glue per service.

## When REST is the right call

- **Public / partner / browser APIs.** gRPC-web exists but adds a proxy and friction; JSON is the lingua franca for external consumers.
- **Small org / few services / low traffic.** The operational tax of gRPC (tooling, proto build pipeline, debugging) can outweigh the benefits. `curl` + JSON is just easier.
- **Heavy human debuggability needs.** JSON is readable on the wire; protobuf needs tooling to inspect.
- **Polyglot with weak gRPC support** in some language you depend on (rare now, but check).

## Honest trade-offs to weigh

| Factor | gRPC | REST |
|---|---|---|
| Wire efficiency | ✅ binary/HTTP2 | ❌ verbose JSON |
| Contract enforcement | ✅ compile-time | ⚠️ OpenAPI, runtime-ish |
| Debuggability / curl-ability | ❌ needs tooling | ✅ trivial |
| Browser support | ❌ needs gRPC-web proxy | ✅ native |
| Streaming | ✅ native | ⚠️ awkward |
| Ecosystem / onboarding | ⚠️ steeper | ✅ everyone knows it |

## Bottom line

- **More than ~5–10 services, performance-sensitive, polyglot, you own both ends → gRPC internally, REST at the edge.** This "gRPC inside, REST outside" pattern is the common mature-architecture default.
- **Small team, few services, mostly CRUD, or external consumers → REST.** Don't pay the gRPC tax until you feel the pain it solves.

If you tell me your service count, language mix, and whether latency is actually a constraint, I'll give you a firmer call rather than the general one.
F:
Recommendation: use gRPC for internal service-to-service calls, and keep REST/JSON at your edges (public APIs, browser clients, webhooks, third-party integrations). This "gRPC inside, REST at the border" split is the mainstream default for a reason — but it's conditional, and I'd flip it for small teams. Reasoning below.

**Why gRPC wins for internal traffic**

- **Enforced contracts.** Protobuf is a schema-first IDL: the `.proto` file *is* the contract, and codegen produces typed clients/servers in every language. This kills the silent drift you get when a producer changes a JSON field and three consumers find out in production. For internal services that change often, this discipline is the biggest practical payoff — bigger than performance.
- **Performance that compounds at scale.** Binary serialization (smaller payloads) plus HTTP/2 multiplexing (many concurrent calls over one connection) means lower latency and higher throughput than REST/JSON. On internal hot paths with high call volume, this is real.
- **First-class streaming, deadlines, cancellation.** Server/client/bidirectional streaming is native, and deadline propagation + cancellation across a call chain is built in — exactly the patterns internal fan-out traffic needs.
- **Polyglot fit.** If your services span Go/Java/Python/etc., consistent generated stubs everywhere is a major win.

**Why REST stays at the edge**

- Universal and zero-friction to consume — any HTTP client works, no codegen.
- Browsers can't speak raw gRPC; gRPC-web needs a proxy (Envoy) and adds moving parts.
- Debuggable by hand: `curl`/Postman just work. gRPC needs `grpcurl` + server reflection.

**The honest caveats — these can flip the decision**

- **Operational cost.** gRPC load balancing is connection-level, so naive L4 balancing pins traffic to one backend; you need client-side LB or a mesh/proxy (Envoy, Linkerd, Istio). Observability tooling is also less plug-and-play than for plain HTTP. If you're not already on a service mesh, budget for this.
- **The contract matters more than the wire format.** A lot of REST's reputation for drift comes from skipping schemas. REST + OpenAPI with generated clients and contract tests gets you most of gRPC's safety with far less operational complexity.

So: **if you're a small team with a handful of services optimizing for velocity and debuggability, just use REST + OpenAPI — don't pay gRPC's complexity tax for throughput you can't yet measure.** Adopt gRPC when you have genuine drivers: polyglot services, high internal call volume, streaming needs, or a mesh already in place. Don't choose gRPC for "performance" in the abstract; choose it when one of those is concretely true.
